gist: Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz argue that the shift from centralized 'old media' to fragmented 'new media' has structurally changed how companies build brand: the brand is now a single durable person (founder/CEO), authenticity beats media-training, and the winning play is 'going direct' by plugging your company into the most interesting story in the world rather than talking about yourself. They frame legacy press as structurally adversarial since ~2017 and unrecoverable.

insights.claim: Old media imposed restricted channels and formats where the corporate brand was the company; new media offers unlimited formats and channels where the brand is now a person, making founder-led personal branding structurally necessary rather than optional.
insights.speaker: Ben Horowitz
insights.so_what: Companies whose public identity is an abstract corporate brand rather than a durable individual are structurally disadvantaged in attention markets; weight founder communication ability when assessing a company's go-to-market.
insights.quote: the companies that are winning in marketing, the brand is the person

insights.claim: The corporate abstract brand (e.g. General Motors, Procter & Gamble brands, conglomerates) was an artifact of centralized media from the 1930s-40s onward, where messages had to be distilled to an 'atomic unit' to pass through a narrow straw of three networks and a few front pages; as centralized media collapses, that model is unwinding.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: Frames personal-brand-led marketing as a durable structural reversion, not a fad, implying CMO playbooks and agency models built for the centralized era are being repriced.
insights.quote: I think that was because of the rise of centralized media specifically

insights.claim: The brand-carrying person must be a permanent fixture of the organization (founder or co-founder), not a hired VP of marketing on a three-year run, because a transient spokesperson breaks the model.
insights.speaker: Ben Horowitz
insights.so_what: Succession and key-person risk around a founder-brand becomes a material company risk to underwrite; a CEO who is also the brand is hard to replace without value destruction.
insights.quote: It can't be like the vice president of marketing who's here for a three-year run and then is gone. Like that will never work.

insights.claim: Andreessen claims that around 2017 the press's 'speak truth to power' mission decisively swamped its 'objective journalism' mission, and that this shift is permanent and will never unwind, making favorable coverage through traditional media structurally unattainable.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: Treat legacy-press strategy as a dead end for tech narrative; budget and trust should shift to owned and allied channels.
insights.quote: there is no way um to get to anything resembling a story that you're going to like through the traditional media anymore. Like it's just... basically not possible.

insights.claim: You can land an individual favorable story in legacy media but you cannot run a sustained communications strategy through it; favorable cases are 'the exception that proves the rule' against 99 unfavorable ones.
insights.speaker: Eric / Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: Distinguishes one-off PR wins from strategy; founders banking on legacy press for ongoing narrative control are misallocating effort.
insights.quote: And you can land a story, but you can't run a strategy

insights.claim: Even Washington political insiders, long assumed to be the last holdout reading legacy outlets, now primarily consume new media such as the Mark Halperin newsletter rather than Axios or the Washington Post.
insights.speaker: Ben Horowitz
insights.so_what: Removes the last commonly-cited exception for maintaining legacy-press relationships; even policy/regulatory influence campaigns can be run through new media.
insights.quote: it's the Mark Halperin newsletter, um which is new media

insights.claim: The highest-leverage media coaching is to get a principal to say publicly exactly what they would say to a friend over lunch about a topic they know intimately, rather than the classic media-training drills of pivots and minimizing controversy.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: A concrete, transferable communications heuristic for coaching founders; cuts against the entire legacy media-training industry.
insights.quote: we're going to get you to basically just say all the things in public that you would say if you were sitting having lunch with a friend

insights.claim: Legacy CEOs measured a successful interview by minimum controversy and 'making no news,' which is precisely the wrong objective in new media where being interesting is the goal.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: Spotting a 'no-news, buttoned-up' communicator at the top of a company signals a marketing ceiling; interesting, polarizing founders are the asset.
insights.quote: they they they they rank their success... based on minimum minimum controversy

insights.claim: People trained for 10 years in old media find it very hard to transition to new media because the rules are 'opposite world,' so hiring experienced old-media marketers for new-media work is a common and costly mistake.
insights.speaker: Ben Horowitz
insights.so_what: When evaluating a company's marketing org, prior big-PR pedigree can be a negative signal; look instead for hires who have personally built an audience.
insights.quote: if you're trained in old media, it's very very very hard to do new media

insights.claim: The core hiring filter for a new-media team is proof of work: has the candidate personally built a brand and an audience, regardless of other skills, because audience-building is the end-to-end competency that matters.
insights.speaker: Ben Horowitz
insights.so_what: Actionable diligence/hiring heuristic; reframes marketing talent evaluation around demonstrated audience creation rather than agency credentials.
insights.quote: the best thing on new media is have you done it? Have you kind of built some brand on something and some audience.

insights.claim: Multiple world-class storytellers on a team is a major power boost because elite storytelling ability is far above average and largely innate, and most old-marketing people lack it because they relied on the principal for the story.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: Storytelling depth on a comms team is a scarce, differentiating asset worth paying up for and hard to replicate.
insights.quote: if you can build a team that's got multiple storytellers that are good, that's a huge power boost

insights.claim: The new-media playbook is offense-oriented (telling your own story through owned and allied channels), in deliberate contrast to old media's defensive posture rooted in the rule that 'you can never take back anything.'
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen / Ben Horowitz
insights.so_what: Reframes corporate comms from risk-minimization to proactive narrative-setting; a defensive comms posture now signals strategic weakness.
insights.quote: Old media is defense oriented. New media is offense.

insights.claim: Public attacks should be treated as brand-building opportunities to assert your own position, but only when responding improves your position rather than amplifying a low-audience critic; a meaningful share of a16z's brand was built by responding to attackers.
insights.speaker: Ben Horowitz
insights.so_what: Picking high-value fights is a deliberate growth lever; watch for founders who can weaponize controversy versus those who get baited.
insights.quote: fights are good for brand building. Um but you just got to pick the right fight to not just build somebody else's brand.

insights.claim: Once a brand reaches a certain size, attracting strong haters is inevitable and desirable; the failure mode is being lukewarm and neutral, because you cannot be interesting without people both loving and hating you.
insights.speaker: Ben Horowitz
insights.so_what: Polarization is a feature of a successful brand; sentiment 'balance' is a misleading metric and softening to be liked degrades marketing.
insights.quote: nobody writes a puff piece on like Rupert Murdoch or Elon Musk... When you get to a certain size, people hate you. Um, and that's good

insights.claim: Andreessen frames going direct not merely as a tactic but as a responsibility for tech leaders, given that the profound and complex changes in tech are hard to understand from outside amid high environmental noise.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: Signals a16z will keep escalating owned-media output (posts, podcasts) as a deliberate institutional strategy, not ad hoc PR.
insights.quote: this is like I think a responsibility that for people in our world and the people in tech now to do

insights.claim: The cheat code for founder communication is to plug the company into the single most interesting story happening in the world rather than talking about the company itself, since the inside-out 'me and my product' story is undifferentiated and boring.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: A repeatable narrative framework investors can use to assess whether a founder will command attention; watch for outside-in vs navel-gazing positioning.
insights.quote: Think in terms of like what are the most interesting things happening in the world and then... how do those things relate to us?

insights.claim: Alex Karp is the exemplar of outside-in positioning: he almost never describes what Palantir does (only 'ontology and orchestration'), instead attaching Palantir to big interesting stories like US military, superintelligence, and neurodivergence, which makes him the first phone call when those topics break.
insights.speaker: Ben Horowitz / Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: Illustrates how narrative positioning translates into being the default media voice on a category, an intangible moat for enterprise/government sales.
insights.quote: he never talks about Palantir. The only thing he ever says about Palantir... is ontology and orchestration, two words that nobody knows what they mean.

insights.claim: Ryan Petersen grew Flexport's profile by talking about the global supply chain collapsing during COVID rather than about freight, which earned him a 60 Minutes appearance and implicit 'by Flexport' brand attribution without naming the product.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: Second concrete proof that category-level narrative ownership during a macro event drives outsized brand and enterprise-sales returns.
insights.quote: the difference between talking about freight versus talking about the global supply chain is completely collapsing during COVID

insights.claim: For enterprise and government sales, the operative question is whether the founder is important enough to get in the room with the decision maker (customer CEO, Secretary of War, White House, Fortune 500 CEOs); talking about the big issues is the 'catnip' that earns that access.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: Links narrative strategy directly to enterprise pipeline and access, a tangible revenue mechanism for evaluating founder-led GTM.
insights.quote: are you important enough to like meet with the CEO of your customer?

insights.claim: Roughly 0.0001% of Palantir investors have read the S-1/10-K filings while ~100% have seen Alex Karp on YouTube, so time spent on dense legal disclosure documents is dramatically lower-leverage than building a clear outside-in story.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen / Ben Horowitz
insights.so_what: Implies investor relations and equity narrative are increasingly driven by founder media presence rather than filings, relevant to how public-company sentiment forms.
insights.quote: What percentage of the investors in you know, Palantir have read the S1 K1... Like... 0.0001% What percentage have seen Alex you know, on YouTube doing his thing?

insights.claim: Founders systematically over-index on distribution and tactics (going viral, getting on Joe Rogan) before getting the message right, but distribution is only a multiplier on the message, so amplifying a wrong or boring message broadcasts that you are uninteresting.
insights.speaker: Gabby
insights.so_what: A diagnostic for founder comms maturity; chasing reach before message is a red flag that applies equally to new and old media.
insights.quote: Distribution is really just a multiplier on the message. And so if the message is wrong, now you've amplified something that is either irrelevant... or... uninteresting.

insights.claim: Successful companies struggle most with messaging because everything they could say is true, so the discipline is to start from the desired outcome (which customer or engineer you want, what they need to believe) and work backwards rather than saying everything at once.
insights.speaker: Gabby
insights.so_what: An outcome-driven messaging method investors can use to test whether a founder's narrative is strategic versus a true-but-unfocused data dump.
insights.quote: Just because everything is true doesn't mean it's all relevant or strategic for your business.

insights.claim: Since 2024, going on a three-hour Joe Rogan-style podcast and being able to talk about anything is now table stakes for presidential candidates, with the consensus democratic retrospective being that Kamala Harris's failure to appear was a mistake.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen
insights.so_what: Signals long-form personal media is now a hard requirement for top-tier leadership, raising the communication bar for politicians and CEOs alike.
insights.quote: 100% of the people who work in that world now think that the next person is going to have to go on Rogan.

insights.claim: New-media communication is a learnable, cultivatable skill, not just innate talent, evidenced by how much Alex Karp, Palmer Luckey, Elon Musk and even Donald Trump (restrained in 1980s interviews) improved versus their early appearances.
insights.speaker: Marc Andreessen / Ben Horowitz
insights.so_what: A founder weak at media today is not permanently capped; coachability of communication is a developable lever worth investing in.
insights.quote: If you look at Donald Trump's interviews in the 80s they're very old media interviews. He's actually restrained.

insights.claim: The comms team must be able to push back on and improve the message before distributing it; a team with no opinion on message produces weak output even if individually skilled at distribution.
insights.speaker: Ben Horowitz
insights.so_what: Org-design implication: build comms teams invested in message-shaping, not order-takers, when assessing a company's marketing function.
insights.quote: if the team is kind of doesn't have an opinion on message, that's very difficult
